title: R’a-beni-lütfen
text:
R’a-beni-lütfen

Enes Furkan Altın 2220469011 https://github.com/altinefurkan
Mehmet Öksüzoğlu 2220469036 (github)
Arda Yeniçulha 2220469061 (github linkiniz)

Project Title: The Quantitative Impact of USD/TRY Fluctuations on Borsa Istanbul (BIST) Indices.
Project Description: This study aims to investigate the correlation between USD/TRY exchange rate movements and the performance of various sectoral indices in Turkey. We are specifically analyzing whether currency volatility serves as a significant driver for returns in the BIST 100, Electricity (XU:ELEKT), and Industrial (XU:SIN) sectors. #Data Set This study is built upon a comprehensive financial dataset spanning a 20-year period from January 2006 to May 2026. The primary variables analyzed include:
USD/TRY Exchange Rate: The core indicator used to measure currency volatility and macroeconomic stability.
BIST 100 Index (XU100.IS): The main benchmark for the overall performance of the Turkish equity market.
Sectoral Indices: BIST Banking (XBANK.IS) and BIST Industrials (XUSIN.IS) were selected to evaluate varying sectoral sensitivities to currency shocks.
Source & Selection: While the team initially attempted to utilize the Central Bank (TCMB) EVDS system, we pivoted to Yahoo Finance due to the technical complexities of EVDS API integration for a two-decade scope.
Data Preprocessing: Raw time series were cleaned through inner joins to ensure aligned trading days, filtered for missing values (NAs), and transformed into daily log returns for robust statistical analysis. #Key Takeaways Long-Term Comparative Scope: This study analyzes the quantitative relationship between the USD/TRY exchange rate and Borsa Istanbul (BIST) indices across a 20-year span (2006–2026), utilizing 5-year analysis windows to identify structural shifts in market behavior.
The 2011–2020 Depreciation Cycle: During the 2011–2015 and 2016–2020 periods, significant currency depreciation (as shown by the USD/TRY parity doubling and tripling) was generally correlated with negative USD-denominated returns, with the Banking sector consistently being the most vulnerable to exchange rate shocks.
Structural Divergence (2021–2026): In a historical anomaly, the most recent period (2021–2026) shows that despite the USD/TRY parity increasing six-fold (from 100 to over 600), BIST indices provided a robust hedge against hyper-depreciation, yielding positive USD-denominated returns for the first time in a decade.
Sectoral Sensitivity and Resilience: While the Industrial index (XUSIN.IS) typically offered more stability across all cycles, the Banking sector (XBANK.IS) displayed extreme volatility but showed a surprising recovery in USD terms during the 2021–2026 period.
Investment Implications: The analysis concludes that while exchange rate spikes historically triggered market sell-offs, the current economic regime has transformed the Turkish equity market into a primary vehicle for capital preservation against extreme currency volatility.

title: Analysis
text:
#Key Takeaways Long-Term Comparative Scope: This study analyzes the quantitative relationship between the USD/TRY exchange rate and Borsa Istanbul (BIST) indices across a 20-year span (2006–2026), utilizing 5-year analysis windows to identify structural shifts in market behavior.
The 2011–2020 Depreciation Cycle: During the 2011–2015 and 2016–2020 periods, significant currency depreciation (as shown by the USD/TRY parity doubling and tripling) was generally correlated with negative USD-denominated returns, with the Banking sector consistently being the most vulnerable to exchange rate shocks.
Structural Divergence (2021–2026): In a historical anomaly, the most recent period (2021–2026) shows that despite the USD/TRY parity increasing six-fold (from 100 to over 600), BIST indices provided a robust hedge against hyper-depreciation, yielding positive USD-denominated returns for the first time in a decade.
Sectoral Sensitivity and Resilience: While the Industrial index (XUSIN.IS) typically offered more stability across all cycles, the Banking sector (XBANK.IS) displayed extreme volatility but showed a surprising recovery in USD terms during the 2021–2026 period.
Investment Implications: The analysis concludes that while exchange rate spikes historically triggered market sell-offs, the current economic regime has transformed the Turkish equity market into a primary vehicle for capital preservation against extreme currency volatility.
#Correlation Matrix (full period) cat(“?????? Full-Period Log-Return Correlation Matrix ??????”) cor_mat <- cor(return_data[, ret_cols], use = “complete.obs”) rownames(cor_mat) <- colnames(cor_mat) <- gsub(“^Ret_”, ““, rownames(cor_mat)) print(round(cor_mat, 4))
usd_cor_row <- grep(“USD.TRY”, rownames(cor_mat), value = TRUE)[1] cat(“>> USD/TRY correlations with BIST indices:”) usd_cors <- sort(cor_mat[usd_cor_row, ], decreasing = TRUE) print(round(usd_cors[names(usd_cors) != usd_cor_row], 4)) The matrix below shows the overall 20-year correlation between USD/TRY returns and BIST indices.

Period-Based Analysis

2006-20102011-20152016-20202021-Present

title: Data
text:
#PACKAGE BOOTSTRAP required_pkgs <- c(“quantmod”, “tidyverse”, “lubridate”, “patchwork”, “scales”)
invisible(lapply(required_pkgs, function(pkg) { if (!requireNamespace(pkg, quietly = TRUE)) { message(“Installing:”, pkg) install.packages(pkg, repos = “https://cloud.r-project.org”, quiet = TRUE) } }))
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(quantmod) library(tidyverse) library(lubridate) library(patchwork) library(scales) })
cat(“All packages loaded.”) #PARAMETERS START_DATE <- as.Date(“2006-01-01”) END_DATE <- Sys.Date()
TICKERS <- c( “TRY=X”, # USD/TRY exchange rate “XU100.IS”, # BIST 100 “XBANK.IS”, # BIST Banking “XUSIN.IS” # BIST Industrials )
LABELS <- c( “TRY=X” = “USD/TRY”, “XU100.IS” = “BIST 100”, “XBANK.IS” = “BIST Banking”, “XUSIN.IS” = “BIST Industrials” ) #TIME PERIODS <- list( list(label = “2006-2010”, start = as.Date(“2006-01-01”), end = as.Date(“2010-12-31”)), list(label = “2011-2015”, start = as.Date(“2011-01-01”), end = as.Date(“2015-12-31”)), list(label = “2016-2020”, start = as.Date(“2016-01-01”), end = as.Date(“2020-12-31”)), list(label = “2021-Present”, start = as.Date(“2021-01-01”), end = END_DATE) #DATA FETCHING fetch_one <- function(ticker, from, to) { env <- new.env() result <- suppressWarnings( tryCatch( getSymbols(ticker, src = “yahoo”, from = from, to = to, env = env, auto.assign = TRUE, warnings = FALSE), error = function(e) NULL ) ) if (is.null(result)) { warning(sprintf(“Could not fetch %s. Skipping.”, ticker)) return(NULL) } xts_obj <- get(ticker, envir = env) adj_col <- grep(“Adjusted”, colnames(xts_obj), value = TRUE) if (length(adj_col) == 0) return(NULL)
col_name <- gsub(“[^A-Za-z0-9]”, “_”, ticker) tibble(Date = as.Date(index(xts_obj)), !!col_name := as.numeric(xts_obj[, adj_col])) }
cat(“full history from Yahoo Finance…”)
raw_list <- lapply(TICKERS, function(tk) { cat(sprintf(” Fetching %-12s … “, tk)) df <- fetch_one(tk, START_DATE, END_DATE) if (!is.null(df)) cat(sprintf(”%d rows“, nrow(df))) else cat(”FAILED“) df }) names(raw_list) <- TICKERS raw_list <- Filter(Negate(is.null), raw_list)
if (length(raw_list) < 2) stop(“Fewer than 2 assets fetched. Cannot proceed.”) #CLEAN THE DATASET
price_data <- purrr::reduce(raw_list, inner_join, by = “Date”) |> arrange(Date) |> drop_na()

Rename columns to friendly ASCII labels using LABELS map
ticker_cols <- gsub(“[^A-Za-z0-9]”, “_”, names(LABELS)) # e.g. “TRY_X” friendly_names <- unname(LABELS) # e.g. “USD/TRY” # Only rename cols that actually exist after merge for (i in seq_along(ticker_cols)) { if (ticker_cols[i] %in% colnames(price_data)) { colnames(price_data)[colnames(price_data) == ticker_cols[i]] <- friendly_names[i] } }
price_cols <- setdiff(colnames(price_data), “Date”) usd_try_col <- price_cols[grepl(“USD/TRY”, price_cols, fixed = TRUE)] bist_cols <- price_cols[price_cols != usd_try_col]
cat(sprintf(“Full merged data: %d rows x %d columns (%s to %s)”, nrow(price_data), ncol(price_data), min(price_data\(Date), max(price_data\)Date))) #LOG RETURNS (full dataset)
return_data <- price_data |> mutate(across(all_of(price_cols), ~ log(. / lag(.)), .names = “Ret_{.col}”)) |> slice(-1) |> drop_na()
ret_cols <- grep(“^Ret_”, colnames(return_data), value = TRUE) usd_ret_col <- ret_cols[grepl(“USD.TRY”, ret_cols)][1] bist_ret_cols <- ret_cols[ret_cols != usd_ret_col]


Download the final processed dataset (.RData)

title: The Quantitative Impact of USD/TRY Fluctuations on Borsa Istanbul (BIST) Indices
text: This study aims to investigate the correlation between USD/TRY exchange rate movements and the performance of various sectoral indices in Turkey. We are specifically analyzing whether currency volatility serves as a significant driver for returns in the BIST 100 (XU100.IS), Banking (XBANK.IS), and Industrial (XUSIN.IS) sectors.

title: The Quantitative Impact of USD/TRY Fluctuations on Borsa Istanbul (BIST) Indices
text:
Data Set
This study is built upon a comprehensive financial dataset spanning a 20-year period from January 2006 to May 2026. The primary variables analyzed include:

USD/TRY Exchange Rate: The core indicator used to measure currency volatility and macroeconomic stability.
BIST 100 Index (XU100.IS): The main benchmark for the overall performance of the Turkish equity market.
Sectoral Indices: BIST Banking (XBANK.IS) and BIST Industrials (XUSIN.IS) were selected to evaluate varying sectoral sensitivities to currency shocks.


Source & Selection: While the team initially attempted to utilize the Central Bank (TCMB) EVDS system, we pivoted to Yahoo Finance due to the technical complexities of EVDS API integration for a two-decade scope.
Data Preprocessing: Raw time series were cleaned through inner joins to ensure aligned trading days, filtered for missing values (NAs), and transformed into daily log returns for robust statistical analysis.

title: The Quantitative Impact of USD/TRY Fluctuations on Borsa Istanbul (BIST) Indices
text:
Key Takeaways

1. Long-Term Comparative Scope
This study analyzes the quantitative relationship between the USD/TRY exchange rate and Borsa Istanbul (BIST) indices across a 20-year span (2006–2026), utilizing 5-year analysis windows to identify structural shifts in market behavior.


2. The 2011–2020 Depreciation Cycle
During the 2011–2015 and 2016–2020 periods, significant currency depreciation (as shown by the USD/TRY parity doubling and tripling) was generally correlated with negative USD-denominated returns, with the Banking sector consistently being the most vulnerable to exchange rate shocks.


3. Structural Divergence (2021–2026)
In a historical anomaly, the most recent period (2021–2026) shows that despite the USD/TRY parity increasing six-fold, BIST indices provided a robust hedge against hyper-depreciation, yielding positive USD-denominated returns for the first time in a decade.


4. Sectoral Sensitivity and Resilience
While the Industrial index (XUSIN.IS) typically offered more stability across all cycles, the Banking sector (XBANK.IS) displayed extreme volatility but showed a surprising recovery in USD terms during the 2021–2026 period.


5. Investment Implications
The analysis concludes that while exchange rate spikes historically triggered market sell-offs, the current economic regime has transformed the Turkish equity market into a primary vehicle for capital preservation against extreme currency volatility.
